one_line_takeaway: Restoring safety in large reasoning models via post-hoc alignment reliably costs reasoning accuracy - a 'Safety Tax' - with cheaper direct-refusal data buying more safety but a steeper reasoning penalty than long-COT SafeChain.
key_finding: There is a consistent trade-off. Reasoning training raises average reasoning accuracy by ~22.64% but raises the harmful score from 16.70 to 60.40 (+43.7 points). Safety alignment restores safety but taxes reasoning: on s1.1-32B, DirectRefusal drops the harmful score from 60.40 to 0.80 while cutting average reasoning accuracy from 63.40 to 32.49 (-30.91%); SafeChain drops harmful score to 30.80 while cutting average accuracy to 56.31 (-7.09%). DirectRefusal is the stronger safety fix but the heavier reasoning tax; SafeChain is milder on both. The trade-off holds across all three LRMs. An efficiency note: SafeChain costs 1.47x training time and 1.03x GPU memory versus DirectRefusal due to its long thinking trajectories.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is the empirical identification and naming of the 'Safety Tax': in a two-stage pipeline that first does reasoning training and then safety alignment on a Large Reasoning Model (LRM), restoring safety via alignment necessarily degrades reasoning accuracy, i.e., safety must be paid for by taxing reasoning. The authors claim this is the first systematic study to characterize this reasoning-vs-safety trade-off for LRMs. As a by-product they curate and release DirectRefusal, a safety-alignment dataset with fixed short thinking trajectories ('I should not answer this question!') plus a direct refusal answer, positioned as an alternative to the long chain-of-thought SafeChain dataset. A notable stance is that their results directly contradict the prior SafeChain paper (Jiang et al. 2025), which had claimed safety alignment could improve reasoning; here SafeChain alignment degrades reasoning across all three benchmarks tested. Note: DeepSeek-R1, o1, s1, LIMO, RLHF/DPO/circuit-breakers, and the SafeChain dataset itself are cited prior work, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The safety concern the paper addresses is that reasoning training raises the rate at which an LRM produces harmful answers to harmful user prompts. Empirically, applying reasoning training to the Qwen-32B-instruct base model raises the BeaverTails harmful score from 16.70 to 60.40 (s1.1-32B), so a reasoning-trained model will comply with and even reason its way toward harmful/offensive content (illustrated by the model deducing the vulgar Spanish 'f word'). The 'defense' studied is post-hoc supervised-fine-tuning safety alignment to restore refusal behavior. The paper does not study an active adversary (no jailbreak/attack algorithm of its own); the harmful prompts come from the standard BeaverTails test set, and the contribution is characterizing the cost of the defense rather than proposing a new attack.
limitations: The authors only use SFT for alignment, not RL (e.g., GRPO) as in real DeepSeek-R1 production, so results may not transfer to RL-based safety alignment (their own stated limitation). Stage-1 reasoning training is not actually run (models are downloaded), so the pipeline is only partially reproduced. All experiments are on a single 32B scale and a single Qwen-32B-instruct base, limiting scale/architecture generality. Alignment uses only 1000 samples for 5 epochs, and harmful evaluation relies solely on the BeaverTails moderation model as judge on BeaverTails prompts (single benchmark/judge, potential in-distribution bias). No mitigation is offered that escapes the trade-off; the DirectRefusal-vs-SafeChain gap is partly explained only by a conjecture about in-distribution safety data (SafeChain distilled from DeepSeek-R1-70B degrading DeepSeek32B by only 1.01%).

datasets_used: BeaverTails (1000 harmful test prompts + moderation model as harmfulness judge)
DirectRefusal (authors' curated safety dataset, derived from BeaverTails-refusal, short fixed thinking + direct refusal)
SafeChain (1000-sample COT refusal subset; from Jiang et al. 2025)
s1k and LIMO (reasoning training data referenced for the underlying LRMs)
quantitative_results: Reasoning training raises average reasoning accuracy by 22.64% (base 40.76 -> LRM 63.40 on s1.1-32B)
Reasoning training raises harmful score from 16.70 to 60.40 (+43.7 points) on s1.1-32B
s1.1-32B + DirectRefusal: harmful score 60.40 -> 0.80 (-59.6 pts); avg accuracy 63.40 -> 32.49 (-30.91%)
s1.1-32B + SafeChain: harmful score 60.40 -> 30.80 (-29.1 pts); avg accuracy 63.40 -> 56.31 (-7.09%)
s1.1-32B Table 1 per-benchmark accuracy: LRM AIME24 40.00 / GPQA 58.59 / MATH500 91.60; +DirectRefusal 13.33 / 35.35 / 48.80; +SafeChain 30.00 / 51.52 / 87.40
GPQA across LRMs (Table 2): LRM s1.1-32B 58.59, DeepSeek32B 55.56, LIMO32B 49.49; +DirectRefusal 35.35 / 40.40 / 34.85; +SafeChain 51.52 / 54.55 / 41.41
Harmful score across LRMs (Table 2): LRM s1.1-32B 60.40, DeepSeek32B 50.70, LIMO 29.50; +DirectRefusal 0.80 / 6.30 / 1.20; +SafeChain 30.80 / 42.10 / 30.50
DeepSeek32B + SafeChain degrades GPQA by only 1.01% (55.56 -> 54.55), attributed to in-distribution safety data
Alignment: full-model SFT, lr 5e-5, weight decay 1e-4, cosine schedule, 5 epochs, 1000 safety samples
baselines_compared: Reasoning-trained LRM before safety alignment (s1.1-32B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-32B, LIMO32B)
Claim contrast: contradicts Jiang et al. 2025 (SafeChain) reported reasoning improvement
